questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with a 20-year smoking history and poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus is found dead at home. At autopsy, cross-sections of the left anterior descending artery reveal a lesion with a large lipid core covered by a thin fibrous cap. The intima shows numerous macrophage-derived foam cells and cholesterol clefts. Which of the following best describes the earliest event in the development of this lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Platelet aggregation over an eroded fibrous cap

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endothelial dysfunction and increased permeability to lipoproteins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calcification of the arterial media

choices.label: D
choices.text: Formation of a fibrin-rich thrombus in the lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smooth muscle cell migration into the intima

explanation.summary: The earliest step in atherogenesis is endothelial dysfunction, which increases permeability and allows LDL to accumulate in the intima.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endothelial dysfunction, triggered by risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, and hypertension, increases vascular permeability to lipoproteins. LDL enters and is oxidized in the intima, attracting monocytes that become foam cells, forming the fatty streak.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Platelet aggregation over an eroded cap is a late complication triggering acute coronary syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Calcification is a feature of advanced plaques and is not part of early pathogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thrombus formation occurs after plaque rupture and represents an acute complication, not an early event.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smooth muscle cell migration occurs later during fibrous cap formation, not during initiation.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents for a routine physical. She has no history of cardiovascular disease. Her lipid panel shows elevated LDL and low HDL. Her physician discusses her increased cardiovascular risk since menopause. Which of the following best explains the increased atherosclerosis risk after menopause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased hepatic clearance of LDL cholesterol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased thromboxane A2 production by platelets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased platelet reactivity from progesterone withdrawal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced secretion of matrix metalloproteinases

choices.label: E
choices.text: Estrogen deficiency leading to unfavorable cholesterol changes and endothelial dysfunction

explanation.summary: Estrogen deficiency after menopause promotes unfavorable lipid profiles, arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Menopause increases atherosclerosis risk due to estrogen deficiency, which leads to unfavorable changes in cholesterol levels (increased LDL, decreased HDL), increased arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased hepatic LDL clearance would lower LDL and be cardioprotective; the opposite occurs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased thromboxane A2 would reduce platelet aggregation, which would be protective.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Progesterone withdrawal is not the primary mechanism; the key hormonal change is estrogen deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reduced MMP secretion would stabilize plaques; the concern in atherosclerosis is increased MMP activity.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia undergoes coronary angiography after an abnormal stress test. Imaging reveals a 70% stenosis of the left anterior descending artery. A biopsy of the plaque would most likely show which of the following in the intimal layer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concentric medial hypertrophy with onion-skinning

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lipid-laden macrophages, smooth muscle cells, and a fibrous cap

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transmural neutrophilic infiltration with fibrinoid necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amyloid deposits within the vessel wall

explanation.summary: Atherosclerotic plaques contain lipid-laden macrophages (foam cells), migrated smooth muscle cells, and a fibrous cap over a necrotic lipid core.
explanation.whyCorrect: A mature atherosclerotic plaque consists of a lipid-rich necrotic core containing foam cells, smooth muscle cells migrated from the media, extracellular lipid, and a fibrous cap of collagen and smooth muscle cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Concentric medial hypertrophy with onion-skinning is seen in malignant hypertension affecting arterioles.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transmural neutrophilic infiltration with fibrinoid necrosis describes vasculitis such as polyarteritis nodosa.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Caseating granulomas are characteristic of tuberculosis, not atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amyloid deposition in vessel walls occurs in amyloid angiopathy, a separate condition.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines an autopsy specimen from a 55-year-old man who died of sudden cardiac death. The coronary artery shows a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque with overlying thrombus. Which of the following plaque characteristics most increased the risk of rupture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heavy calcification throughout the plaque

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thin fibrous cap with a large lipid core and inflammatory cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Small lipid core with extensive smooth muscle proliferation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intimal fibrosis without significant lipid accumulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thick fibrous cap with dense collagen deposition

explanation.summary: Vulnerable plaques prone to rupture have a thin fibrous cap, large lipid core, and abundant inflammatory cells that secrete matrix-degrading enzymes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Plaques most prone to rupture have a thin fibrous cap and large lipid core. Inflammatory cells and their matrix-degrading enzymes weaken the cap, making it susceptible to rupture, which triggers thrombosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heavy calcification is a feature of advanced stable plaques and does not specifically increase rupture risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Extensive smooth muscle proliferation with a small lipid core describes a stable, fibrotic plaque.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intimal fibrosis without lipid accumulation would not produce a vulnerable plaque.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A thick fibrous cap with dense collagen is characteristic of a stable plaque resistant to rupture.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with no prior cardiac history collapses while jogging. Autopsy reveals a fresh thrombus occluding the right coronary artery at the site of a ruptured plaque. His wife reports he never had chest pain. Which of the following best explains why this patient had no warning symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The thrombus dissolved spontaneously before each episode

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right coronary artery occlusion cannot cause symptoms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary artery disease only becomes symptomatic after age 65

choices.label: D
choices.text: The plaque caused less than 70% stenosis before rupture, insufficient to produce exertional angina

choices.label: E
choices.text: He had variant angina that only occurs during sleep

explanation.summary: Plaques causing less than 70% stenosis typically do not produce exertional symptoms, yet can still rupture and cause sudden thrombotic occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Many patients are asymptomatic because their plaques have not yet narrowed the lumen enough (>70%) to cause demand ischemia. However, these non-flow-limiting plaques can be vulnerable and rupture suddenly to cause complete occlusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Spontaneous thrombus dissolution does not explain chronic absence of symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right coronary artery occlusion causes inferior MI with chest pain, bradycardia, and hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atherosclerotic events can occur at any adult age, especially with multiple risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Variant angina causes rest pain from vasospasm and would not explain asymptomatic plaque rupture.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with smoking, hypertension, and diabetes presents with intermittent claudication in his left leg that worsens with walking and improves with rest. Examination reveals a diminished dorsalis pedis pulse. Which vascular territory is most likely affected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lower extremity arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carotid arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mesenteric arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary arteries

explanation.summary: Intermittent claudication with diminished pedal pulses indicates peripheral arterial disease from atherosclerosis of the lower extremity arteries.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atherosclerosis commonly affects the lower extremity arteries, causing peripheral arterial disease. Intermittent claudication with diminished pedal pulses is the classic presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Carotid artery atherosclerosis presents with TIAs or stroke symptoms, not leg pain.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renal artery atherosclerosis presents with secondary hypertension or renal insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mesenteric artery atherosclerosis causes postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atherosclerosis does not typically affect pulmonary arteries.

questions.stem: A researcher studies oxidized LDL in atherogenesis. Monocytes migrate into the arterial intima and engulf oxidized LDL particles. Which of the following cell types do these monocytes become within the vessel wall?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endothelial progenitor cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mast cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibroblasts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macrophages that become foam cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vascular smooth muscle cells

explanation.summary: Monocytes entering the intima differentiate into macrophages, which engulf oxidized LDL via scavenger receptors to become foam cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: After migrating into the intima, monocytes differentiate into macrophages that take up oxidized LDL through scavenger receptors, becoming lipid-laden foam cells. These are the hallmark of the fatty streak.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Endothelial progenitor cells are not derived from monocyte differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mast cells arise from distinct bone marrow precursors and are not a major component of early lesions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibroblasts are connective tissue cells not derived from monocytes in atherogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smooth muscle cells migrate from the media but are not derived from monocytes.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with transient ischemic attacks undergoes carotid duplex ultrasonography, which reveals bilateral carotid stenosis. Atherosclerosis in the cerebral vasculature most commonly leads to which clinical outcome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Communicating hydrocephalus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Berry aneurysm rupture and subarachnoid hemorrhage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ischemic stroke from thromboembolism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intracerebral hemorrhage from vessel wall rupture

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis

explanation.summary: Atherosclerosis of the carotid and cerebral arteries most commonly leads to ischemic stroke through thromboembolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carotid and cerebral artery atherosclerosis is a major cause of ischemic stroke. Plaque rupture with thrombosis or embolization of fragments to distal cerebral vessels causes downstream ischemia and infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Communicating hydrocephalus results from impaired CSF reabsorption, not atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Berry aneurysms are congenital saccular aneurysms unrelated to atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atherosclerosis predominantly causes ischemic rather than hemorrhagic stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis involves the venous system and is caused by hypercoagulability.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with familial hypercholesterolemia has a coronary artery calcium score of 450. His physician prescribes a high-intensity statin. In addition to lowering LDL cholesterol, which of the following is another beneficial effect of statin therapy on atherosclerotic plaques?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversibly inhibiting platelet aggregation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dissolving existing thrombi in coronary arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the size of the lipid core

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stabilizing plaques by reducing inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Promoting plaque calcification and rupture

explanation.summary: Statins stabilize atherosclerotic plaques through anti-inflammatory effects, in addition to lowering LDL cholesterol.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beyond lowering LDL, statins stabilize plaques by reducing inflammation, reversing endothelial dysfunction, and decreasing thrombogenicity, helping prevent plaque rupture.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Statins are not antiplatelet agents; aspirin and P2Y12 inhibitors serve that role.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Statins do not dissolve thrombi; thrombolytic agents such as tPA serve that function.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins reduce rather than increase the lipid core by lowering circulating LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Statins stabilize plaques; they do not promote rupture.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis and no traditional cardiovascular risk factors is found to have coronary artery disease on CT angiography. Which of the following best explains the association between her condition and atherosclerosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated ESR increasing blood viscosity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate therapy causing direct coronary toxicity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic systemic inflammation accelerating endothelial dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct bacterial infection of the coronary arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rheumatoid factor deposits within the intima

explanation.summary: Rheumatoid arthritis is a risk factor for atherosclerosis because chronic systemic inflammation accelerates endothelial dysfunction and plaque development.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic inflammatory conditions like rheumatoid arthritis promote endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and inflammatory processes driving plaque formation, independent of traditional risk factors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Elevated ESR and viscosity are not the primary mechanism linking RA to atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate does not cause coronary toxicity and may reduce CV risk by controlling inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atherosclerosis is not caused by bacterial infection of coronary arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rheumatoid factor does not deposit within coronary intima.

questions.stem: A physician states that atherosclerosis begins silently during childhood. A student asks at what age the process typically becomes clinically evident. Which of the following is the most accurate response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In middle age or later

choices.label: B
choices.text: During adolescence (ages 13-18)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Before age 10 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: In early adulthood (ages 20-30)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only after age 70 years

explanation.summary: Although atherosclerosis begins in childhood, it usually becomes clinically evident in middle age or later.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atherosclerotic plaque development begins in childhood with fatty streaks. Clinical manifestations typically do not appear until middle age or later, when plaques limit flow or become unstable.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Early lesions are present in adolescence, but symptomatic disease is exceedingly rare.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fatty streaks may be present before age 10, but clinical disease does not manifest.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clinically evident disease in early adulthood is uncommon except with familial hypercholesterolemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Many patients develop clinical disease well before age 70, especially with risk factors.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with coronary artery disease undergoes abdominal aortic ultrasonography. The scan reveals an infrarenal aortic diameter of 5.8 cm. Which of the following is the most serious complication of this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aneurysm rupture with exsanguination

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilateral renal artery stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic mesenteric ischemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic valve stenosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic coarctation

explanation.summary: The most serious complication of an abdominal aortic aneurysm is rupture, which can cause fatal hemorrhage.
explanation.whyCorrect: An infrarenal aortic diameter of 5.8 cm represents a significant AAA. As diameter increases, rupture risk rises. Rupture causes massive hemorrhage and is frequently fatal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Renal artery stenosis is a separate condition; this aneurysm is infrarenal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mesenteric ischemia is not the most serious direct complication of AAA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic valve stenosis involves the aortic valve and is not a complication of AAA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic coarctation is a congenital narrowing, not a complication of atherosclerotic aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with episodic postprandial abdominal pain and a 15-pound weight loss over 3 months. He has a long history of smoking and peripheral arterial disease. Mesenteric angiography shows significant stenosis of the superior mesenteric artery. Which of the following is the underlying pathologic process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fibromuscular dysplasia of the mesenteric arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atherosclerotic narrowing of the mesenteric vasculature

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombosis of the portal venous system

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasculitis of medium-sized mesenteric vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Median arcuate ligament compression syndrome

explanation.summary: Postprandial pain and weight loss in a patient with atherosclerotic risk factors is classic for chronic mesenteric ischemia from atherosclerosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intestinal angina with postprandial pain, weight loss, and food avoidance in a patient with smoking and PAD is classic for chronic mesenteric ischemia from atherosclerotic narrowing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibromuscular dysplasia typically affects renal and carotid arteries in young women.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Portal venous thrombosis presents with acute abdominal pain, ascites, and splenomegaly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vasculitis is possible but far less common than atherosclerosis in this demographic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Median arcuate ligament syndrome is rare and typically seen in younger patients.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a family history of premature coronary artery disease asks about his most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis. Which of the following is the most important independent predictor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heavy alcohol consumption

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated serum homocysteine level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Family history of premature cardiovascular disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sedentary lifestyle

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-carbohydrate diet

explanation.summary: Family history (genetics) is the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Family history is the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis. Genetic predisposition to premature cardiovascular disease significantly increases risk regardless of modifiable factors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heavy alcohol consumption is a minor risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Elevated homocysteine is a minor risk factor, not the most important independent predictor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sedentary lifestyle is a modifiable minor risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-carbohydrate diet is a minor risk factor.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man reports squeezing chest pain for 3 months that occurs only during his morning jog and radiates to his left arm. The pain resolves within 5 minutes of stopping exercise. His resting ECG is normal. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of his symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fixed coronary stenosis causing demand ischemia during exertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pericardial inflammation causing friction-related chest pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Microvascular dysfunction from small vessel inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plaque rupture with superimposed thrombus formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coronary artery vasospasm reducing myocardial perfusion at rest

explanation.summary: Stable angina results from fixed atherosclerotic coronary stenosis that limits blood flow during increased myocardial oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: In stable angina, a fixed atherosclerotic plaque narrows the coronary artery. At rest, blood flow is adequate. During exertion, the heart demands more oxygen, but the stenosed vessel cannot increase supply, causing ischemia and chest pain that resolves with rest.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pericarditis causes pleuritic chest pain that worsens with inspiration and improves leaning forward.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Microvascular dysfunction can cause angina but is not the classic mechanism for exertional chest pain relieved by rest.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plaque rupture with thrombus formation causes unstable angina or MI, presenting with rest pain or escalating symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coronary vasospasm characterizes vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, which occurs at rest, not with exertion.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden-onset heavy midsternal chest pain at rest lasting 25 minutes. She has hyperlipidemia and peripheral vascular disease. ECG shows ST-segment depressions in leads V4-V6. Serum troponin I measured by a traditional assay is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute pericarditis

choices.label: B
choices.text: ST-elevation myocardial infarction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stable angina pectoris

choices.label: E
choices.text: Unstable angina

explanation.summary: New-onset rest pain with ST depressions and negative traditional troponin is most consistent with unstable angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has new-onset severe chest pain at rest, ST-segment depressions on ECG, and negative troponin by traditional assay. This constellation is classic for unstable angina, which results from plaque rupture with subtotal thrombotic occlusion. High-sensitivity troponin assays might detect minor myocardial injury.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pericarditis shows diffuse ST elevations, pleuritic pain, and a friction rub.
whyWrongByChoice.B: STEMI shows ST-segment elevations, not depressions, and troponin would be positive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GERD causes burning pain with a sour taste, relieved by antacids, without ECG changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stable angina occurs predictably with exertion and resolves with rest; this patient has rest pain.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman who smokes heavily presents with recurrent episodes of chest pain that awaken her from sleep at 3 AM. The pain responds promptly to sublingual nitroglycerin. An ECG obtained during an episode shows transient ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. Coronary angiography reveals no obstructive lesions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Unstable angina from plaque rupture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stable angina from fixed coronary stenosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina

choices.label: E
choices.text: Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: Recurrent rest angina with transient ST elevations, response to nitrates, smoking history, and clean coronary angiography are classic for vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vasospastic angina results from intermittent coronary artery spasm unrelated to atherosclerosis. It typically occurs at rest (often at night), shows transient ST elevations during episodes, responds to nitrates, and has normal coronary angiography. Smoking is a major risk factor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Takotsubo presents with acute chest pain mimicking MI, apical ballooning, and emotional stress trigger.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Unstable angina from plaque rupture would typically show ST depressions and coronary stenosis on angiography.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stable angina occurs with exertion, not at rest, and is caused by fixed atherosclerotic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NSTEMI shows ST depressions or T-wave inversions and positive troponin, not transient ST elevations with clean coronaries.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with stable angina is started on sublingual nitroglycerin for acute symptom relief. He returns complaining of severe headaches after each use. Which of the following best explains this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reflex tachycardia increasing intracranial pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis in the brain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased venous return to the heart causing cerebral congestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct stimulation of pain receptors in the meninges

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasodilation of cerebral blood vessels by cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin headache occurs because the drug causes vasodilation of cerebral vessels through cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitroglycerin releases nitric oxide, which increases cGMP and causes vascular smooth muscle relaxation. This vasodilation affects cerebral vessels, causing headache in over 60% of patients. It is the most common adverse effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While reflex tachycardia can occur, the headache is caused by direct cerebral vasodilation, not increased intracranial pressure from tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prostaglandin synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of NSAIDs, not nitroglycerin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroglycerin decreases venous return (venodilation reduces preload), not increases it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin does not directly stimulate meningeal pain receptors; the headache results from vascular dilation.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with stable angina controlled on metoprolol and sublingual nitroglycerin asks which of his medications will reduce his risk of dying from cardiovascular disease. His physician adds atorvastatin to his regimen. Which of the following best describes the mortality benefit of this addition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atorvastatin reduces mortality by decreasing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atorvastatin reduces mortality by directly vasodilating coronary arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atorvastatin reduces mortality by lowering LDL and stabilizing coronary plaques

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atorvastatin reduces mortality by inhibiting platelet aggregation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atorvastatin has no mortality benefit in stable angina

explanation.summary: Statins reduce cardiovascular mortality in stable angina by lowering LDL cholesterol and stabilizing atherosclerotic plaques.
explanation.whyCorrect: Statins provide primary prevention for MI and reduce deaths from cardiac disease. They work by reducing LDL cholesterol levels and stabilizing coronary artery plaques, reducing the risk of plaque rupture and subsequent MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreasing myocardial oxygen demand is the mechanism of beta-blockers, not statins.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Statins do not directly vasodilate coronary arteries; nitrates provide that effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Statins do not inhibit platelet aggregation; that is the role of aspirin and P2Y12 inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Statins have proven mortality benefit in patients with stable angina and coronary artery disease.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with suspected stable angina undergoes exercise stress testing. During the treadmill test, her ECG shows 3-mm ST-segment depressions in leads V3-V6, and she reports typical chest pain. Her blood pressure drops from 140/85 mmHg to 125/80 mmHg during peak exercise. Which of the following stress test findings in this patient is most indicative of high-risk coronary artery disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Achieving target heart rate without symptoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exercise-induced hypotension with large ST depressions and chest pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: An increase in blood pressure during peak exercise

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mild ST elevations resolving immediately after exercise

choices.label: E
choices.text: ST depressions limited to a single lead

explanation.summary: Exercise-induced hypotension combined with large ST depressions and concurrent angina indicates high-risk coronary artery disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: A positive exercise stress test includes large ST depressions (>2-3 mm), concurrent angina, and exercise-induced hypotension (drop of >10 mmHg). These findings together indicate high-risk disease and a large amount of at-risk myocardium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Achieving target heart rate without symptoms suggests a negative or low-risk test.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood pressure normally rises during exercise; an appropriate increase is a normal response.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mild ST elevations resolving immediately are nonspecific and not typical of high-risk disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ST depressions in a single lead are less concerning than multi-lead depressions.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe osteoarthritis of both knees and suspected stable angina needs further evaluation. He cannot walk on a treadmill. His baseline ECG shows left bundle branch block. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resting 12-lead ECG

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repeat lipid panel and hemoglobin A1c

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pharmacologic stress test with nuclear imaging

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercise electrocardiography on a treadmill

choices.label: E
choices.text: 24-hour Holter monitor

explanation.summary: Pharmacologic stress testing with imaging is appropriate when the patient cannot exercise and has a baseline ECG abnormality that precludes exercise ECG interpretation.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient cannot exercise (severe bilateral knee OA) and has a left bundle branch block making exercise ECG interpretation unreliable. Pharmacologic stress testing with nuclear imaging or echocardiography bypasses both limitations by using a vasodilator or dobutamine to induce stress and imaging to detect ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A resting ECG is already abnormal (LBBB) and will not provoke or detect ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lab tests assess risk factors but do not diagnose coronary ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Exercise ECG is not feasible due to his inability to exercise, and LBBB makes ECG interpretation unreliable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A Holter monitor detects arrhythmias but is not designed to diagnose coronary artery disease.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with stable angina on maximal medical therapy continues to have symptoms. Coronary angiography reveals severe stenosis of the left main coronary artery. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the dose of sublingual nitroglycerin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adding a calcium channel blocker to his regimen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Watchful waiting with serial stress testing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery bypass grafting

choices.label: E
choices.text: Percutaneous coronary intervention with stent placement

explanation.summary: Left main coronary artery disease is an indication for coronary artery bypass grafting rather than PCI due to the risk of catastrophic stent occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: CABG is preferred for left main coronary artery disease because stenting in this location carries a risk of stent occlusion that would deprive the entire left coronary circulation of blood, potentially causing fatal MI. CABG provides more durable revascularization for this anatomy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing nitroglycerin dose will not address the fixed stenosis in a patient already on maximal medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Adding a CCB may provide symptom relief but does not address the high-risk anatomy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Watchful waiting is inappropriate for symptomatic left main disease, which carries high mortality risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PCI with stenting of the left main coronary is problematic because stent occlusion would deprive the entire left coronary circulation.

questions.stem: A physician explains to a medical student that beta-blockers are first-line therapy for stable angina. The student asks how beta-blockers relieve anginal symptoms. Which of the following best describes the mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreasing heart rate and contractility, thereby reducing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocking late sodium current to reduce intracellular calcium overload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct coronary artery vasodilation increasing oxygen supply

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibiting platelet aggregation to prevent thrombus formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reducing LDL cholesterol and stabilizing atherosclerotic plaques

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers reduce angina by decreasing heart rate and contractility through beta-1 receptor blockade, lowering myocardial oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-blockers inhibit beta-1 receptors on the heart, producing negative chronotropic (decreased heart rate) and negative inotropic (decreased contractility) effects. This reduces myocardial oxygen demand, preventing the supply-demand mismatch that causes anginal symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blocking late sodium current is the mechanism of ranolazine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-blockers do not directly vasodilate coronary arteries; that is the role of nitrates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet aggregation inhibition is the mechanism of aspirin and P2Y12 inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LDL reduction and plaque stabilization are effects of statins.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with vasospastic angina asks about long-term management. His physician recommends smoking cessation and a first-line medication. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy for vasospastic angina?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atorvastatin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metoprolol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diltiazem

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clopidogrel

explanation.summary: Calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem are first-line therapy for vasospastic angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: For vasospastic angina, calcium channel blockers (e.g., diltiazem, amlodipine) are first-line because they directly relax coronary artery smooth muscle, preventing the vasospasm that causes the condition. Nitroglycerin is a backup but has tolerance issues with long-term use.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Statins reduce cholesterol and stabilize plaques but do not address coronary vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent used for atherosclerotic disease prevention, not first-line for vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-blockers are first-line for stable angina but are not preferred for vasospastic angina and may worsen vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet agent, not indicated as first-line for vasospastic angina.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man with known stable angina presents to the ED with chest pain that began 2 hours ago while watching television. The pain is more severe than his usual angina and has not responded to three doses of sublingual nitroglycerin. His ECG shows new ST-segment depressions. Which of the following best characterizes the change in his clinical status?

choices.label: A
choices.text: His condition has progressed to unstable angina or acute coronary syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: He has developed vasospastic angina requiring calcium channel blockers

choices.label: C
choices.text: His stable angina is unchanged and he should continue current medications

choices.label: D
choices.text: He has developed pericarditis from viral infection

choices.label: E
choices.text: He is experiencing a panic attack with somatic symptoms

explanation.summary: Worsening chest pain at rest that does not respond to nitroglycerin, with new ECG changes, indicates progression from stable angina to unstable angina/ACS.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient previously had predictable exertional angina. Now he has rest pain, more severe symptoms, failure to respond to nitroglycerin, and new ST depressions. This represents a transition to unstable angina, which is classified as an acute coronary syndrome requiring urgent evaluation and treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vasospastic angina shows transient ST elevations and typically occurs without prior CAD progression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rest pain unresponsive to NTG with new ECG changes is a significant change from stable angina.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericarditis presents with pleuritic pain, friction rub, and diffuse ST elevations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Panic attacks do not cause ST-segment depressions on ECG.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with stable angina has a resting ECG that is normal. Her physician considers ordering a stress test. Which of the following patient populations benefits most from exercise stress testing for diagnosis of coronary artery disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patients with a low pretest probability of coronary artery disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients with acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients with a very high pretest probability of coronary artery disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with ongoing unstable angina symptoms

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patients with an intermediate pretest probability of coronary artery disease

explanation.summary: Exercise stress testing is most useful in patients with intermediate pretest probability of CAD, where the test can meaningfully change the post-test probability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stress testing is most useful in patients with intermediate likelihood of CAD, as determined by history and physical exam. In this group, test results meaningfully shift the probability of disease. Low-risk patients have high false-positive rates, and high-risk patients may have false-negatives.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Low pretest probability patients have a high false-positive rate, making the test less useful.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Exercise stress testing is contraindicated in acute STEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Very high pretest probability patients may have false-negatives and often proceed directly to angiography.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Exercise stress testing is contraindicated during ongoing unstable angina.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with stable angina is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for acute chest pain relief. Which of the following best describes the primary hemodynamic mechanism by which nitroglycerin relieves angina?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing myocardial contractility to improve cardiac output

choices.label: B
choices.text: Venodilation decreasing preload and reducing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Selective arteriolar dilation increasing systemic blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct inhibition of the cardiac conduction system to slow heart rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blocking sympathetic nervous system activation at the adrenal glands

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin primarily relieves angina through venodilation, which decreases preload and reduces myocardial oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitroglycerin produces cGMP-mediated vascular smooth muscle relaxation, targeting veins more than arteries. Venodilation decreases venous return and left ventricular preload, reducing wall tension and myocardial oxygen consumption. It also causes some direct coronary artery dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nitroglycerin does not increase contractility; it reduces cardiac workload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroglycerin causes vasodilation and can decrease blood pressure, not increase it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin does not directly affect the cardiac conduction system.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitroglycerin acts on vascular smooth muscle, not the sympathetic nervous system.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with stable angina has been taking aspirin 81 mg daily. He develops a gastric ulcer and his gastroenterologist recommends discontinuing aspirin. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative antiplatelet agent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ticagrelor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enoxaparin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warfarin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heparin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clopidogrel

explanation.summary: Clopidogrel is an appropriate alternative antiplatelet agent when aspirin is contraindicated in patients with stable angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: In patients with stable angina who cannot take aspirin, clopidogrel is considered an appropriate alternative. It is a P2Y12 inhibitor that blocks ADP-mediated platelet activation and is recommended when aspirin is contraindicated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ticagrelor is typically reserved for acute coronary syndrome settings rather than stable angina monotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant, not an antiplatelet alternative.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Warfarin is an anticoagulant (vitamin K antagonist), not an antiplatelet agent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heparin is a parenteral anticoagulant used for acute thrombotic events, not long-term antiplatelet therapy.

questions.stem: A 57-year-old man presents to the ED with 1 hour of continuous pressing chest pain that began while watching television. ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of his condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased myocardial oxygen demand during physical exertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Partial occlusion of a coronary artery by a stable atheroma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombosis of a coronary artery after plaque rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasospasm of a coronary artery at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Complete occlusion of a coronary artery by a stable atheroma

explanation.summary: STEMI results from complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery following rupture of a vulnerable atherosclerotic plaque.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has a STEMI with ST elevations in inferior leads. STEMI occurs when an atherosclerotic plaque with a thin fibrous cap ruptures, exposing tissue factor and collagen to blood. This triggers platelet aggregation and thrombus formation that completely occludes the coronary artery.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The patient was at rest watching TV, so increased oxygen demand from exertion is not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Partial occlusion by a stable atheroma causes stable angina or NSTEMI, not STEMI with ST elevations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary vasospasm is uncommon and typically causes transient ST changes that resolve with nitrates.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stable atheromas cause gradual stenosis leading to stable angina; they do not typically cause acute complete occlusion.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with crushing chest pain. ECG shows ST elevations in V1-V4. Troponin I is elevated. A diagnosis of anterior wall STEMI is made. Which of the following statements best describes the difference between STEMI and NSTEMI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: STEMI is caused by partial occlusion of a coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: NSTEMI always requires emergent cardiac catheterization

choices.label: C
choices.text: STEMI involves transmural necrosis from complete coronary occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with NSTEMI do not require hospital admission

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only STEMI produces positive troponin results

explanation.summary: STEMI involves transmural (full-thickness) myocardial necrosis from complete coronary occlusion, distinguishing it from NSTEMI.
explanation.whyCorrect: STEMI is caused by complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery, leading to transmural (full-thickness) necrosis. NSTEMI involves incomplete occlusion with nontransmural (subendocardial) infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: STEMI is caused by complete, not partial, occlusion of a coronary artery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: NSTEMI does not always require emergent catheterization; it is typically done within 24-48 hours.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NSTEMI patients require admission for monitoring, anticoagulation, and possible intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both STEMI and NSTEMI produce positive troponin results because both involve myocardial necrosis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with diabetes mellitus presents to the ED with 4 hours of nausea, epigastric discomfort, and dyspnea. She denies chest pain. ECG shows ST depressions in leads V4-V6. Troponin I is elevated. Which of the following best explains why she did not present with typical chest pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: She is having a pulmonary embolism, not an MI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Women, elderly patients, and diabetics often have atypical MI presentations

choices.label: C
choices.text: NSTEMI never causes chest pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epigastric pain always indicates gastroesophageal reflux

choices.label: E
choices.text: Her troponin elevation is from a non-cardiac cause

explanation.summary: Women, elderly patients, and those with diabetes frequently present with atypical MI symptoms such as dyspnea, nausea, and epigastric pain instead of classic chest pain.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atypical MI presentations including dyspnea, nausea, epigastric pain, and fatigue without classic chest pain are common in women, elderly patients, and diabetics. Diabetic neuropathy may blunt cardiac pain perception.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The ECG findings and elevated troponin are consistent with NSTEMI, not PE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: NSTEMI can cause chest pain, but atypical presentations are common in certain populations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epigastric pain can be a presentation of MI, especially in the populations described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While troponin can rise in other conditions, the combination of symptoms and ECG changes supports MI.

questions.stem: A biopsy of myocardial tissue is obtained from a patient who had an MI 2 days ago. Microscopic examination most likely shows which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coagulative necrosis with neutrophilic infiltrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Caseating necrosis with multinucleated giant cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Collagen fibers and mature scar formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coagulative necrosis without inflammatory infiltrate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Normal myocardial histology

explanation.summary: At 1-3 days post-MI, histology shows coagulative necrosis with a prominent neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Between 1-3 days after MI, the histologic hallmark is extensive coagulative necrosis (hyper-eosinophilic cells with preserved outlines but lost nuclei) accompanied by a maximal neutrophilic infiltrate recruited to clean up cellular debris.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Caseating necrosis with giant cells is seen in tuberculosis, not MI.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Collagen and scar formation develop over weeks to months, not at 2 days.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coagulative necrosis without inflammation is seen in the first 4-24 hours, before neutrophils arrive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Normal histology may be present only in the first few hours post-MI, not at 2 days.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is recovering in the hospital 5 days after an anterior wall STEMI. He suddenly develops severe hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension. Which of the following is the most likely complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ventricular free wall rupture with cardiac tamponade

choices.label: C
choices.text: Development of a mural thrombus with systemic embolization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular septal rupture with left-to-right shunt

explanation.summary: Sudden hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and JVD 5 days post-MI suggest ventricular free wall rupture with hemopericardium and cardiac tamponade.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ventricular free wall rupture is a mechanical complication occurring 3-10 days post-MI when the necrotic myocardium is weakened by inflammatory infiltrate but scar tissue has not yet formed. Blood accumulates in the pericardial sac (hemopericardium), causing tamponade with the classic triad of hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and JVD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dressler syndrome causes fever and pericarditis weeks post-MI, not acute tamponade at 5 days.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mural thrombus embolization causes stroke or limb ischemia, not tamponade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Papillary muscle rupture causes acute mitral regurgitation with a new murmur and pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular septal rupture presents with a new holosystolic murmur and heart failure, not tamponade physiology.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with no prior cardiac history presents with acute chest pain after using cocaine at a party. ECG shows ST elevations in leads V1-V3. Troponin is elevated. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how cocaine caused his MI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic fibrosis of the coronary artery media

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autoimmune-mediated coronary artery inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased oxygen demand, coronary vasospasm, and endothelial disruption

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of the coagulation cascade preventing thrombolysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct myocardial toxicity from cocaine metabolites

explanation.summary: Cocaine causes MI through multiple mechanisms: increased myocardial oxygen demand, coronary vasospasm, endothelial disruption, and platelet activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cocaine is a sympathomimetic that increases heart rate and blood pressure (raising oxygen demand), causes coronary vasospasm, disrupts the endothelium, and activates platelets. These combined effects can precipitate acute MI even without pre-existing atherosclerosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cocaine does not cause chronic medial fibrosis; its effects are acute and vascular.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cocaine-induced MI is not autoimmune in nature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cocaine activates platelets and promotes thrombosis; it does not inhibit the coagulation cascade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While cocaine can have toxic effects, the primary mechanism of MI is vascular, not direct myocyte toxicity.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man undergoes successful PCI for an acute STEMI. Two hours later, his troponin levels rise further and the ECG shows new changes. Myocardial biopsy would most likely show contraction band necrosis. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bacterial endocarditis seeding the infarcted myocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reperfusion injury from free radical generation and calcium influx

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stent thrombosis causing a new occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extension of the original infarct due to ongoing ischemia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Allergic reaction to the contrast dye used during PCI

explanation.summary: Contraction band necrosis after successful reperfusion is the hallmark of reperfusion injury caused by oxygen free radicals and calcium influx.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reperfusion injury occurs when blood flow is restored to ischemic myocardium. Returning oxygen generates free radicals that exacerbate damage, and calcium influx causes uncontrolled hypercontraction of myofibers, producing the characteristic contraction band necrosis pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bacterial endocarditis is unrelated to this acute post-PCI presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stent thrombosis is possible but would cause coagulative necrosis, not contraction band necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infarct extension would show coagulative necrosis, not contraction bands, and the PCI was successful.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Contrast allergy causes systemic reactions, not contraction band necrosis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of anterior wall MI 3 months ago presents with progressive exertional dyspnea, bilateral lower extremity edema, and bibasilar crackles. Echocardiogram shows an ejection fraction of 30% with anterior wall akinesis. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his current symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism from deep venous thrombosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute pericarditis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systolic heart failure from loss of contractile myocardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent MI in the right coronary artery territory

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pneumonia with bilateral infiltrates

explanation.summary: Progressive heart failure symptoms months after anterior MI result from loss of contractile myocardium replaced by non-contractile scar tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: After a large anterior MI, the infarcted myocardium is replaced by scar tissue that does not contract. The resulting reduced ejection fraction (30%) leads to systolic heart failure with dyspnea, edema, and pulmonary congestion. Heart failure is now a leading cause of morbidity after MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PE would present with acute dyspnea, pleuritic pain, and tachycardia, not progressive edema.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pericarditis causes pleuritic chest pain and friction rub, not progressive heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Recurrent MI would present acutely with chest pain and new ECG changes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pneumonia would present with fever, productive cough, and unilateral findings.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a heart specimen from a patient who died 6 weeks after an MI. The infarcted area shows fibroblasts, new blood vessels, and collagen deposition. Which of the following terms best describes this histologic finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caseous necrosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mature fibrotic scar

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coagulative necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liquefactive necrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Granulation tissue

explanation.summary: At 1-8 weeks post-MI, the healing myocardium shows granulation tissue characterized by fibroblasts, new blood vessels, and collagen deposition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Between 1-8 weeks after MI, fibroblasts lay down collagen while new blood vessels form (angiogenesis). This combination of fibroblasts, neovascularization, and collagen is called granulation tissue, representing the active healing phase before mature scar formation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caseous necrosis is seen in tuberculosis, not MI.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mature fibrotic scar forms after 8 weeks, with completed collagen deposition and no active fibroblasts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coagulative necrosis is seen in the first few days after MI, not at 6 weeks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Liquefactive necrosis occurs in the brain, not in the heart after MI.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the ED with severe chest pain. An ECG is obtained immediately and shows ST elevations in V1-V4. Stat troponin I drawn on arrival is negative. Which of the following is the best next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Order a chest CT to rule out aortic dissection first

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain an echocardiogram before any treatment decisions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wait 24 hours for troponin to become positive before treating

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discharge the patient because troponin is negative

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diagnose STEMI based on ECG and initiate emergent reperfusion

explanation.summary: In STEMI, the ECG is the critical initial diagnostic tool. Emergent reperfusion should not be delayed for troponin results, which may take 4-6 hours to become positive.
explanation.whyCorrect: ECG is the best initial test for MI. ST-segment elevations with classic chest pain are sufficient to diagnose STEMI and initiate emergent therapy, even with a negative initial troponin. Cardiac biomarkers may not be detectable for 4-6 hours after symptom onset.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While aortic dissection is in the differential, the classic ECG findings and presentation favor STEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Echocardiography should not delay emergent reperfusion in a clear STEMI presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Waiting 24 hours for troponin confirmation in STEMI would be catastrophic; time is myocardium.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A negative troponin on arrival does not rule out MI; serial testing is needed as troponin takes hours to rise.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man is diagnosed with an NSTEMI. His physician explains that the infarction primarily involves the subendocardial myocardium. Which of the following best explains why the subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The subendocardium lacks gap junctions for oxygen diffusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: The subendocardium is farthest from the coronary blood supply

choices.label: C
choices.text: The subendocardium has the fewest mitochondria

choices.label: D
choices.text: The subendocardium has the highest metabolic rate in the heart

choices.label: E
choices.text: The subendocardium contains the cardiac conduction system

explanation.summary: The subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemia because it is the farthest region from the epicardial coronary blood supply.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coronary arteries run along the epicardial surface and penetrate inward. The subendocardial myocardium is the farthest layer from the blood supply, making it the most susceptible to ischemia when coronary blood flow is reduced, as in NSTEMI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gap junctions connect cardiomyocytes electrically, not for oxygen diffusion; their presence does not explain ischemic vulnerability.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The subendocardium does not have fewer mitochondria; its vulnerability relates to blood supply anatomy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While the subendocardium is metabolically active, its vulnerability is primarily due to distance from blood supply.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While the conduction system traverses the subendocardium, this does not explain ischemic vulnerability.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is admitted with an acute anterior wall MI. On hospital day 3, a new holosystolic murmur is heard at the apex with radiation to the axilla. He develops acute pulmonary edema. Which of the following is the most likely mechanical complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular aneurysm formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mural thrombus with systemic embolization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ventricular free wall rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Papillary muscle rupture causing acute mitral regurgitation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Interventricular septum rupture

explanation.summary: A new holosystolic murmur at the apex with acute pulmonary edema 3 days post-MI indicates papillary muscle rupture causing acute mitral regurgitation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papillary muscle rupture is a mechanical complication occurring 2-7 days post-MI when the myocardium is weakened. It causes acute mitral regurgitation presenting as a new holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla, with sudden pulmonary edema and heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LV aneurysm develops over weeks and does not cause an acute murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mural thrombus embolization causes stroke or limb ischemia, not a new murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Free wall rupture causes cardiac tamponade (hypotension, muffled sounds, JVD), not a new murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Septal rupture causes a new holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border, not the apex, with left-to-right shunt.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ED with fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and swollen hands. He is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. His pediatrician is most concerned about which cardiac complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysms with risk of thrombosis and MI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic valve stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease, a medium-vessel vasculitis, characteristically causes coronary artery aneurysms that can lead to thrombosis and MI.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis that primarily affects children. Its most serious complication is coronary artery aneurysms, which can lead to thrombosis, occlusion, and downstream MI. This is why children with Kawasaki disease receive aspirin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic valve stenosis is unrelated to Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary hypertension is not a characteristic complication of Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dilated cardiomyopathy is not the characteristic cardiac complication of Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constrictive pericarditis is associated with tuberculosis and radiation, not Kawasaki disease.

questions.stem: A cardiologist explains the role of cardiac biomarkers in MI diagnosis. CK-MB levels peak earlier and normalize sooner than troponin levels. In which of the following clinical scenarios is CK-MB most useful compared to troponin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Screening asymptomatic patients for coronary artery disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confirming pericarditis as the cause of chest pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diagnosing stable angina in an outpatient setting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detecting reinfarction in a patient who had an MI 4 days ago

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initial diagnosis of a first MI in the emergency department

explanation.summary: CK-MB normalizes sooner than troponin, making it useful for detecting reinfarction (a repeat MI) when troponin may still be elevated from the initial event.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because CK-MB normalizes sooner than troponin (which can remain elevated for 7-10 days), a re-elevation of CK-MB can help detect reinfarction when troponin levels are still elevated from the initial MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neither CK-MB nor troponin is used for screening asymptomatic patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pericarditis may have mildly elevated troponin but CK-MB has no specific role in its diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stable angina does not cause biomarker elevation with traditional assays.
whyWrongByChoice.E: For initial MI diagnosis, troponin is the preferred and more specific biomarker.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man presents with chest pain lasting 45 minutes. His ECG shows ST elevations meeting STEMI criteria in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal depressions in II, III, and aVF. The ST elevation criterion for lead V2 in this patient (male, age >40) requires at least how many millimeters of elevation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0.5 mm

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.5 mm

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.0 mm

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1.0 mm

choices.label: E
choices.text: 2.5 mm

explanation.summary: For anterior leads V2-V3, STEMI criteria require at least 2 mm of ST elevation in men aged 40 and older.
explanation.whyCorrect: A diagnosis of STEMI requires ST elevation in 2 contiguous leads. For anterior leads V2-V3, the threshold varies by age and sex: 2 mm or more in men 40 years or older, 2.5 mm or more in men under 40, and 1.5 mm or more in women regardless of age.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.5 mm is below the threshold for any lead and would not meet STEMI criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 1.5 mm is the criterion for women in leads V2-V3, not men over 40.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 1.0 mm is the criterion for non-anterior leads, not V2-V3 in men.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 2.5 mm is the criterion for men under 40 years in leads V2-V3.

questions.stem: During an MI, the body activates endogenous thrombolysis. Endothelial cells release tissue plasminogen activator (tPA). Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which tPA promotes clot dissolution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: tPA directly inhibits factor Xa in the coagulation cascade

choices.label: B
choices.text: tPA blocks the P2Y12 receptor on platelet membranes

choices.label: C
choices.text: tPA irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase on platelets

choices.label: D
choices.text: tPA neutralizes thromboxane A2 in the vessel lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: tPA converts plasminogen to plasmin, which dissolves fibrin

explanation.summary: tPA converts plasminogen to plasmin, and plasmin dissolves fibrin in the thrombus, thereby promoting clot breakdown.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) released by endothelial cells activates plasminogen to plasmin. Plasmin is a serine protease that cleaves fibrin, the structural protein of the thrombus, thereby dissolving the clot and restoring blood flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Factor Xa inhibition is the mechanism of fondaparinux and heparin, not tPA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: P2Y12 receptor blockade is the mechanism of clopidogrel and ticagrelor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cyclooxygenase inhibition is the mechanism of aspirin, not tPA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thromboxane A2 neutralization is not the mechanism of tPA.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with crushing chest pain and ECG findings consistent with an inferior STEMI. He is given aspirin, metoprolol, and heparin. The interventional cardiologist is preparing the catheterization lab. Within what time frame should PCI ideally be performed after symptom onset for a STEMI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Within 30 minutes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within 7 days

choices.label: C
choices.text: Within 90 minutes of pain onset, up to 12 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Within 24 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Within 48 hours

explanation.summary: For STEMI, PCI with stent placement should be performed within 90 minutes of symptom onset and can be beneficial up to 12 hours after onset.
explanation.whyCorrect: In STEMI, time is myocardium. PCI with stent placement within 90 minutes is the goal, and revascularization can still be beneficial up to 12 hours after symptom onset. Any delay decreases treatment effectiveness as the area of necrosis expands progressively.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 30 minutes is the goal for thrombolytic administration when PCI is unavailable, not the PCI target.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Waiting 7 days for a STEMI would result in extensive irreversible myocardial damage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 24 hours is acceptable for NSTEMI angiography, not STEMI where urgency is much greater.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 48 hours is the upper limit for NSTEMI intervention, not STEMI.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with suspected STEMI arrives at a rural hospital without catheterization capabilities. The nearest PCI-capable center is 3 hours away by ground transport. Helicopter transport is unavailable due to weather. Which of the following is the most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer IV GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors as definitive therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform emergent coronary artery bypass grafting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give sublingual nitroglycerin and discharge home

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start dual antiplatelet therapy and wait for transfer

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer IV tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)

explanation.summary: When timely PCI is unavailable for STEMI, IV thrombolytics (tPA) should be administered within 30 minutes of first medical contact.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thrombolytic agents such as tPA are used as a backup reperfusion strategy for STEMI when PCI is not readily available. They should be given within 12 hours of symptom onset, ideally within 30 minutes of first medical contact.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors are adjuncts to PCI, not definitive reperfusion therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CABG is not an emergent reperfusion strategy for acute STEMI at a rural hospital.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Discharging a STEMI patient is inappropriate and potentially fatal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dual antiplatelet therapy alone does not achieve reperfusion; waiting 3+ hours wastes critical time.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man presents with an acute STEMI. As part of initial treatment, he is given chewable aspirin 325 mg. Which of the following best explains why the aspirin tablet is chewed rather than swallowed whole?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chewing allows faster absorption and more rapid platelet inhibition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chewing enhances the anticoagulant properties of aspirin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chewing prevents aspirin from being metabolized by the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chewing converts aspirin to its active metabolite

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chewing reduces the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding

explanation.summary: Chewing aspirin in STEMI allows faster buccal and gastric absorption, enabling more rapid platelet inhibition.
explanation.whyCorrect: In STEMI, rapid platelet inhibition is critical. Chewing or crushing the aspirin tablet increases surface area and allows faster absorption through the buccal and gastric mucosa, achieving therapeutic blood levels and platelet inhibition more quickly than swallowing whole.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent, not an anticoagulant; chewing does not change its mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chewing does not prevent hepatic first-pass metabolism; it simply speeds absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin does not require conversion to an active metabolite; it directly and irreversibly inhibits COX.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chewing does not reduce GI bleeding risk; it simply accelerates absorption.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old man admitted for unstable angina is started on standard ACS medications including heparin. On day 5, his CBC shows a platelet count that has dropped from 258,000/mcL on admission to 50,000/mcL. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this hematologic finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clopidogrel-induced aplastic anemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin-induced bone marrow suppression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Statin-induced thrombocytopenia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Metoprolol-induced platelet destruction

explanation.summary: A significant drop in platelet count 5 days into heparin therapy is classic for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT).
explanation.whyCorrect: HIT is an immune-mediated response to heparin that causes platelet destruction and a dropping platelet count, typically occurring 5-10 days after initiation. It paradoxically causes hypercoagulability despite thrombocytopenia and requires immediate heparin discontinuation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Clopidogrel can cause bleeding but does not typically cause aplastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin causes GI bleeding but does not suppress bone marrow or cause isolated thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Statins cause myalgia and hepatotoxicity, not thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metoprolol causes bradycardia and hypotension, not platelet destruction.

questions.stem: A patient with ACS is being treated with clopidogrel. A medical student asks the attending about the mechanism of this drug. Which of the following best describes how clopidogrel works?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase on platelets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibits thrombin by binding to antithrombin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activates plasminogen to dissolve existing thrombi

choices.label: D
choices.text: Irreversibly blocks the ADP (P2Y12) receptor on platelets

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reversibly binds the GP IIb/IIIa receptor on platelets

explanation.summary: Clopidogrel is an irreversible P2Y12 (ADP receptor) antagonist that prevents ADP-mediated platelet activation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Clopidogrel irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 receptor on platelets. By preventing ADP binding, it inhibits the platelet activation pathway, reducing platelet aggregation and thrombus formation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Irreversible COX inhibition is the mechanism of aspirin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Binding antithrombin is the mechanism of heparin, not clopidogrel.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Plasminogen activation is the mechanism of tPA and streptokinase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GP IIb/IIIa receptor antagonists include abciximab and eptifibatide, not clopidogrel.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with STEMI is given nitroglycerin sublingually in the ED. His chest pain improves. Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of nitroglycerin in reducing his ischemic chest pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreasing cardiac contractility directly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Venodilation reducing preload and myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dissolving the coronary thrombus directly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreasing afterload by arteriolar dilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreasing heart rate by vagal stimulation

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin primarily works by venodilation, which reduces venous return and preload, thereby decreasing myocardial wall tension and oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitroglycerin produces cGMP-mediated relaxation of vascular smooth muscle, preferentially dilating veins. Venodilation reduces venous return and preload, decreasing ventricular filling, wall tension, and myocardial oxygen demand. It also directly dilates coronary arteries to a lesser degree.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nitroglycerin does not directly decrease cardiac contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroglycerin does not dissolve thrombi; thrombolytic agents serve that function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some afterload reduction occurs, venodilation and preload reduction is the primary mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitroglycerin does not stimulate the vagus nerve or directly reduce heart rate.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a confirmed NSTEMI is being managed medically. His physician considers anticoagulation with heparin. How long should heparin typically be continued for NSTEMI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: For 48 hours or until revascularization, whichever comes first

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heparin is not used in NSTEMI

choices.label: C
choices.text: For 12 hours only

choices.label: D
choices.text: For 6 months post-discharge

choices.label: E
choices.text: Indefinitely as outpatient therapy

explanation.summary: For NSTEMI, heparin is given for 48 hours or until revascularization (PCI) occurs, whichever comes first.
explanation.whyCorrect: In NSTEMI, heparin prevents thrombus propagation while definitive treatment is planned. It is typically continued for 48 hours or until PCI is performed, whichever is first. Heparin is not used indefinitely due to bleeding risks and HIT.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Heparin is used in NSTEMI to prevent thrombus growth.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 12 hours is too short for adequate anticoagulation coverage in NSTEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 6 months of heparin would be excessive and increase bleeding risk and HIT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heparin is not given indefinitely; long-term antiplatelet therapy replaces it.

questions.stem: A 57-year-old man with STEMI undergoes successful PCI with a drug-eluting stent. He is prescribed dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and prasugrel at discharge. For how long should the P2Y12 inhibitor typically be continued?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At least 12 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1 month

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1 week

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3 months

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only during hospitalization

explanation.summary: P2Y12 inhibitors are typically continued for at least 12 months after an acute coronary event with stent placement.
explanation.whyCorrect: After ACS with stent placement, P2Y12 inhibitors are initially used in the acute phase and then continued for at least 12 months to prevent stent thrombosis and recurrent ischemic events.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 1 month is insufficient for optimal protection after ACS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 1 week is too short and risks stent thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 3 months is shorter than the recommended minimum duration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stopping at discharge provides inadequate long-term protection.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with ACS is being considered for thrombolytic therapy with tPA. He had an ischemic stroke 2 weeks ago. Which of the following best describes the appropriateness of thrombolytic therapy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: tPA is only contraindicated if the stroke was hemorrhagic

choices.label: B
choices.text: tPA can be given at a reduced dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: tPA is safe and should be given immediately

choices.label: D
choices.text: tPA is absolutely contraindicated due to recent ischemic stroke

choices.label: E
choices.text: tPA should be combined with heparin to reduce stroke risk

explanation.summary: Recent ischemic stroke is an absolute contraindication to thrombolytic therapy due to high risk of hemorrhagic conversion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Absolute contraindications to thrombolytics include recent ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, active bleeding, aortic dissection, closed head trauma, and severe uncontrolled hypertension. A recent stroke increases the risk of hemorrhagic conversion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both recent ischemic and hemorrhagic strokes are contraindications to thrombolytics.
