whyWrongByChoice.B: Dose reduction does not overcome an absolute contraindication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: tPA is not safe in this patient due to the absolute contraindication of recent stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Combining with heparin would increase, not decrease, bleeding risk.

questions.stem: An attending physician warns a medical student that the traditional MONA mnemonic (morphine, oxygen, nitrates, aspirin) for ACS management is outdated. Of these four agents, which is the only one with proven mortality benefit in ACS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Morphine

choices.label: B
choices.text: All four have proven mortality benefit

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxygen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nitroglycerin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aspirin

explanation.summary: Of the MONA agents, only aspirin has proven mortality benefit in ACS. Morphine, oxygen, and nitrates may cause harm.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspirin is the only MONA agent with proven mortality benefit. Morphine has been associated with decreased survival and interference with antiplatelet drug absorption. Oxygen therapy has no benefit in non-hypoxic patients and may cause harm. Nitrates relieve symptoms but have no mortality benefit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Morphine may decrease survival and mask ischemic pain, an important clinical marker.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Only aspirin has proven mortality benefit; the others may be harmful.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oxygen has no benefit in non-hypoxic MI patients and may cause harm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin relieves pain but has no proven mortality benefit.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) presents with NSTEMI requiring anticoagulation and PCI. Which of the following is the most appropriate anticoagulant alternative?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enoxaparin (low-molecular-weight heparin)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin at a higher dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bivalirudin (direct thrombin inhibitor)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unfractionated heparin at a reduced dose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Warfarin

explanation.summary: Bivalirudin, a direct thrombin inhibitor, is the preferred anticoagulant alternative for patients with HIT undergoing PCI.
explanation.whyCorrect: In patients with a history of HIT, all forms of heparin are absolutely contraindicated. Bivalirudin is a reversible direct thrombin inhibitor that provides effective anticoagulation during PCI without triggering HIT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Enoxaparin is a heparin derivative and is also contraindicated in HIT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent, not an anticoagulant replacement for heparin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Any dose of unfractionated heparin is contraindicated in patients with HIT history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin has a slow onset and is not suitable for acute anticoagulation during PCI.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with NSTEMI is started on ticagrelor as part of dual antiplatelet therapy. Two days later, he reports significant shortness of breath without evidence of heart failure or pulmonary embolism. Which of the following is the most likely explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Known adverse effect of ticagrelor causing dyspnea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allergic reaction to ticagrelor requiring epinephrine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blocker induced bronchospasm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Worsening myocardial ischemia from stent thrombosis

explanation.summary: Ticagrelor causes significant dyspnea in approximately 15% of patients, a recognized adverse effect distinct from cardiac or pulmonary pathology.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ticagrelor has a known adverse effect of dyspnea occurring in about 15% of patients. This is thought to be related to adenosine-mediated effects. It is not a sign of cardiac decompensation or allergy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease causes nasal polyps and asthma, not isolated dyspnea.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Allergic reactions would include urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis, not isolated dyspnea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blocker bronchospasm is possible but less likely without wheezing or asthma history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stent thrombosis would present with chest pain and ECG changes, not isolated dyspnea.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with STEMI undergoes PCI. The cardiologist places a drug-eluting stent. Which of the following best describes the advantage of drug-eluting stents over bare-metal stents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drug-eluting stents dissolve after 6 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug-eluting stents do not require antiplatelet therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drug-eluting stents have a larger lumen diameter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug-eluting stents reduce neointimal proliferation and restenosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drug-eluting stents are less thrombogenic acutely

explanation.summary: Drug-eluting stents release medications that limit neointimal proliferation, reducing the risk of in-stent restenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Drug-eluting stents have a drug bonded to a polymer coating that inhibits neointimal proliferation (smooth muscle cell growth) within the stent. This significantly reduces the rate of restenosis compared to bare-metal stents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Drug-eluting stents are permanent metallic implants; they do not dissolve.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug-eluting stents still require dual antiplatelet therapy, often for a longer duration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lumen diameter depends on stent sizing, not whether it is drug-eluting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Drug-eluting stents may actually require longer antiplatelet therapy due to delayed endothelialization.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old woman with STEMI is being treated with multiple medications. She has no hypoxemia (oxygen saturation 98% on room air). A nurse asks whether supplemental oxygen should be administered. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low-flow nasal cannula is mandatory for all ACS patients

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxygen is not indicated and may cause harm in non-hypoxic patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer high-flow oxygen to all MI patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxygen should be given only after successful reperfusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oxygen is needed to enhance the effectiveness of nitroglycerin

explanation.summary: Supplemental oxygen should only be used in MI patients with hypoxemia (oxygen saturation <90%). It has no benefit and may cause harm in non-hypoxic patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Research shows that supplemental oxygen in non-hypoxic MI patients has no benefit and may cause harm. Oxygen should only be administered when saturation falls below 90%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nasal cannula oxygen is not mandatory for all ACS patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Routine high-flow oxygen for all MI patients is not recommended and may be harmful.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxygen timing is based on saturation level, not reperfusion status.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxygen does not enhance nitroglycerin effectiveness.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with chronic hypertension presents with sudden-onset tearing chest pain radiating to his back. A chest X-ray reveals a mediastinal width of 9 cm. ECG is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mediastinal lymphoma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abdominal aortic aneurysm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anterior mediastinal thymoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Traumatic aortic disruption

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic dissection

explanation.summary: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back with a widened mediastinum in a hypertensive patient is classic for aortic dissection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aortic dissection presents with sudden, severe, tearing chest pain radiating to the back in patients with chronic hypertension. A widened mediastinum (>8 cm) on chest X-ray supports this diagnosis. The normal ECG helps exclude MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lymphoma can cause mediastinal widening but does not present with sudden severe tearing pain.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Abdominal aortic aneurysm does not cause mediastinal widening; it occurs below the diaphragm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thymoma causes mediastinal widening but does not present with acute tearing chest pain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Traumatic aortic disruption occurs after major deceleration injuries, not spontaneously.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old restrained driver is brought to the trauma center after a head-on collision. Chest X-ray shows a widened upper mediastinum and left-sided opacification. Which of the following is the most appropriate next imaging study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent chest CT angiogram

choices.label: B
choices.text: MRI of the chest

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nuclear medicine cardiac stress test

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram

explanation.summary: A widened mediastinum after deceleration injury requires emergent chest CT angiogram to evaluate for traumatic aortic disruption.
explanation.whyCorrect: Widened mediastinum after sudden deceleration is highly concerning for traumatic aortic injury at the ligamentum arteriosum. Emergent chest CT angiogram is the study of choice to confirm or exclude this life-threatening diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: MRI is time-consuming and impractical in the acute trauma setting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Abdominal ultrasound evaluates for intraperitoneal fluid but does not assess the thoracic aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nuclear stress testing is elective and has no role in acute trauma evaluation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Echocardiogram evaluates cardiac function but does not adequately image the thoracic aorta for traumatic injury.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with chronic heart failure presents with worsening dyspnea. His PA chest X-ray shows a cardiothoracic ratio greater than 0.5, prominent hilar vessels, Kerley B lines at the lung periphery, and bilateral pleural effusions. Which of the following findings would be most unexpected in this clinical scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cephalization of pulmonary vessels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hilar vessel enlargement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Edema beginning in the lateral peripheral lung fields

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pleural effusions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cardiomegaly

explanation.summary: In cardiogenic pulmonary edema, edema typically begins centrally near the hilum and radiates outward; peripheral/lateral lung field edema first would be unexpected.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema characteristically starts centrally surrounding the hilar vessels where pulmonary capillary pressure is highest, creating a batwing pattern. The peripheral/lateral lung fields are typically the last to be affected. Edema starting in the lateral fields would be atypical.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cephalization (increased apical vessel visibility) is a classic sign of elevated pulmonary pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hilar vessel enlargement results from increased pulmonary artery pressures and is expected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pleural effusions commonly develop in heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardiomegaly is common in chronic heart failure and is an expected finding.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with new-onset dyspnea. Echocardiography shows a left ventricular end-diastolic volume of 150 mL and an end-systolic volume of 90 mL. What is her ejection fraction, and how does it compare to normal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 40%, which is reduced (normal 55-70%)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75%, which is elevated (normal 55-70%)

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30%, which is severely reduced (normal 55-70%)

choices.label: D
choices.text: 50%, which is borderline (normal 55-70%)

choices.label: E
choices.text: 60%, which is normal (normal 55-70%)

explanation.summary: EF = (EDV - ESV)/EDV = (150 - 90)/150 = 60/150 = 40%, which is below the normal range of 55-70%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ejection fraction = (End Diastolic Volume - End Systolic Volume) / End Diastolic Volume = (150 - 90) / 150 = 40%. A normal EF is 55-70%, so 40% represents a reduced ejection fraction, consistent with systolic heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The calculated EF is 40%, not 75%.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The calculated EF is 40%, not 30%.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The calculated EF is 40%, not 50%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The calculated EF is 40%, not 60%.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with suspected coronary artery disease undergoes stress echocardiography. During dobutamine infusion, the anteroseptal wall of the left ventricle shows hypokinesis while other walls contract normally. Which of the following coronary arteries is most likely diseased?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Posterior descending artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left anterior descending artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right coronary artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left main coronary artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left circumflex artery

explanation.summary: Anteroseptal wall motion abnormality corresponds to the left anterior descending artery territory.
explanation.whyCorrect: Regional wall motion abnormalities on stress echo correspond to specific coronary artery territories. The anteroseptal wall is supplied by the left anterior descending artery (LAD). Hypokinesis in this region during stress indicates LAD stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The posterior descending artery supplies the inferior septum and inferior wall.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The RCA supplies the inferior wall; its disease would cause inferior hypokinesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left main disease would affect both LAD and circumflex territories, causing more widespread abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The left circumflex supplies the lateral wall; its disease would cause lateral hypokinesis.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected pericardial effusion undergoes echocardiography. The study confirms a large effusion with right ventricular diastolic collapse. Which of the following hemodynamic consequences does this finding indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis with restricted filling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac tamponade with impaired venous return

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic valve stenosis with pressure overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitral regurgitation with volume overload

explanation.summary: Right ventricular diastolic collapse on echocardiography indicates cardiac tamponade, where intrapericardial pressure exceeds right-sided chamber pressures.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right ventricular or atrial collapse during diastole on echo indicates that intrapericardial pressure has risen above right-sided chamber pressures, impairing venous return and reducing cardiac output. This is the echocardiographic hallmark of cardiac tamponade.

whyWrongByChoice.A: VSD causes volume overload of the right heart, not chamber collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Constrictive pericarditis shows a thickened pericardium, not chamber collapse from effusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic stenosis causes LV hypertrophy, not RV diastolic collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitral regurgitation causes LA and LV dilation, not RV collapse.

questions.stem: A PA chest X-ray shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette with a globular, symmetric shape resembling a water bottle. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular hypertrophy from chronic hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic root aneurysm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right ventricular enlargement from pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pericardial effusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy

explanation.summary: A globular, symmetric 'water bottle' cardiac silhouette on chest X-ray is characteristic of pericardial effusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pericardial effusion causes symmetric, globular enlargement of the cardiac silhouette due to fluid accumulation within the pericardial sac. This 'water bottle' shape is distinct from the asymmetric enlargement seen with cardiomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LVH causes asymmetric leftward enlargement, not a symmetric globular shape.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic root aneurysm widens the superior mediastinum, not the cardiac silhouette symmetrically.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RV enlargement causes asymmetric rightward enlargement, not a water bottle shape.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dilated cardiomyopathy causes cardiomegaly but typically maintains an asymmetric silhouette.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is referred for screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial screening study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MR angiography of the abdomen

choices.label: B
choices.text: CT angiography of the abdomen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conventional abdominal aortography

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plain abdominal X-ray

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound

explanation.summary: Abdominal ultrasound is the recommended initial screening study for AAA in at-risk populations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ultrasound is the study of choice for AAA screening. It is non-invasive, widely available, inexpensive, radiation-free, and highly accurate for detecting aortic dilation. Screening is recommended for men aged 65-75 with a smoking history.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MRA is accurate but expensive, time-consuming, and not used as an initial screening tool.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT angiography provides excellent detail but involves radiation and contrast; it is reserved for confirmed aneurysms requiring surgical planning.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conventional aortography is invasive and not appropriate for screening.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plain X-ray may show calcification but is insensitive for detecting AAA.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old man presents to the ED with abdominal pain and a blood pressure of 82/35 mmHg. His hemoglobin is 5.7 g/dL. Physical examination is limited by his body habitus. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial imaging study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Point-of-care ultrasound

choices.label: B
choices.text: MRI abdomen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nuclear medicine scan

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conventional angiography

choices.label: E
choices.text: CT abdomen with contrast

explanation.summary: In a hemodynamically unstable patient with suspected ruptured AAA, point-of-care ultrasound at the bedside is the most appropriate initial imaging.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient is hemodynamically unstable with signs of hemorrhagic shock (hypotension, severe anemia). Point-of-care ultrasound is rapid, risk-free, and performed at the bedside. It can quickly confirm the presence of an AAA without transporting the patient to radiology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: MRI is time-consuming, costly, and has no role in emergency abdominal imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nuclear medicine testing is not quick and is inappropriate for this emergency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Conventional angiography is invasive and not appropriate as the initial imaging study.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CT is reasonable if stable, but transporting this unstable patient to radiology is dangerous.

questions.stem: A cardiologist orders a coronary artery calcium (CAC) CT scan for a 55-year-old man with intermediate cardiovascular risk. The report shows a calcium score of 350. Which of the following statements best describes what this test provides?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evaluation of cardiac valve function and regurgitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A numerical risk score based on coronary calcium deposits without evaluating stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessment of myocardial perfusion during stress

choices.label: D
choices.text: Measurement of coronary artery blood flow velocity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct visualization of coronary artery stenosis with contrast

explanation.summary: Coronary artery calcium scoring uses a low-dose non-contrast CT to quantify calcium in coronary plaques as a risk assessment tool, without formally evaluating stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: CAC scoring uses a low-dose, non-contrast CT to measure calcium deposits in coronary artery plaques. It provides a numerical score associated with increased risk of coronary events, guiding management decisions. It does not use contrast and does not evaluate for arterial stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Valve function evaluation is performed by echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocardial perfusion assessment is done by nuclear stress testing, not CAC scoring.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary flow velocity measurement is not a function of CAC scoring.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Direct coronary artery visualization with contrast is CT angiography, not calcium scoring.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with chest pain undergoes conventional coronary angiography (cardiac catheterization). Which of the following is a major advantage of this invasive procedure compared to CT angiography?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides better imaging of the myocardium than the vessels

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has no risk of vascular complications

choices.label: C
choices.text: It requires no contrast dye administration

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can provide simultaneous therapeutic intervention (PCI)

choices.label: E
choices.text: It uses less radiation than a standard chest X-ray

explanation.summary: The major advantage of conventional coronary angiography is the ability to perform simultaneous percutaneous coronary intervention.
explanation.whyCorrect: While conventional coronary angiography is invasive, its major advantage over CT angiography is the ability to perform therapeutic interventions during the same procedure. Areas of critical stenosis can be dilated and stented (PCI), providing both diagnosis and treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Conventional angiography primarily images coronary vessels, not the myocardium.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vascular complications (hematoma, dissection, pseudoaneurysm) are inherent risks of invasive catheterization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conventional angiography requires intra-arterial contrast dye injection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Conventional angiography uses significant radiation, more than a chest X-ray.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with an acute MI undergoes echocardiography. The study shows hypokinesis of the anterolateral wall during systole. In the cardiac ischemic cascade, at what point does regional wall motion abnormality (RWMA) typically develop relative to other signs of ischemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only after complete transmural infarction has occurred

choices.label: B
choices.text: Simultaneously with troponin elevation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Before anginal symptoms and before ECG changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: After troponin elevation but before ECG changes

choices.label: E
choices.text: After anginal symptoms develop but before ECG changes

explanation.summary: Regional wall motion abnormality is the earliest clinical sign in the ischemic cascade, developing within seconds of decreased blood flow, before anginal symptoms and ECG changes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because the myocardium is an obligate aerobe, RWMA (hypokinesis or akinesis) develops within seconds of decreased blood flow/oxygen delivery. This occurs before anginal symptoms develop, before ECG changes of ischemia appear, and before troponin levels rise from myocyte death.

whyWrongByChoice.A: RWMA can be detected with subendocardial ischemia and does not require transmural infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: RWMA occurs much earlier than troponin elevation, which requires cell death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RWMA develops before troponin elevation, not after. Troponin rises after myocyte death, a later event.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RWMA precedes anginal symptoms in the ischemic cascade, not the reverse.
